--- title: "Hip-Hop Periodic Table" subtitle: "A Data Storytelling Teaching Module" author: "Data Storytelling in Professional Practice" format: dashboard: theme: - cosmo - custom.scss logo: "" nav-buttons: - icon: github href: "#" scrolling: true execute: echo: false warning: false message: false --- ::: {.cell} ::: # Overview {.sidebar} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}

Dataset at a Glance

139 artists across 6 eras

7 geographic regions

8 lyrical styles

Confidence breakdown

H: 85 artists
M: 42 artists (provisional)
L: 12 artists (estimates only)

Mean composite score

7.03 / 10

Highest: Kendrick Lamar

Teaching concepts
  • Measurement validity
  • Sampling & bias
  • Multidimensional scoring
  • Composite vs. dimensions
  • Uncertainty communication
  • Visualization design
``` ::: ::: # 1 · What Are We Measuring? ## Row {height="15%"} ::: {.callout-tip title="Teaching Prompt — Construct Validity"} **The central measurement problem:** We started with one variable called "Lyricism" and ended up with five. Was that the right decision? What are we gaining and losing? This is the construct validity question that underlies every KPI, survey instrument, and performance rating in your organization. ::: ## Row {height="85%"} ### Radar: Single artist — five dimensions {width="40%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Scatter: Composite score vs. Storytelling {width="60%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # 2 · Who Is In the Data? ## Row {height="15%"} ::: {.callout-warning title="Teaching Prompt — Sampling Decisions and Bias"} **Every dataset has a frame — and a gap.** This dataset over-represents critically acclaimed artists, NYC, the Golden Age, and men. Those are documented choices. What datasets do you use at work where the frame is undocumented? What populations are missing? ::: ## Row {height="45%"} ### Artists by region {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Gender representation gap {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Row {height="40%"} ### Confidence distribution {width="35%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### What bias looks like in practice {width="65%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # 3 · Dimensions vs. Composite ## Row {height="15%"} ::: {.callout-note title="Teaching Prompt — The Aggregation Problem"} **What does averaging destroy?** Rakim (Rhyme Density 10) and Scarface (Storytelling 10) both score around 8.8 composite. Is that meaningful equivalence — or does the composite erase the most important information? When does aggregation help, and when does it lie? ::: ## Row {height="45%"} ### Heatmap: All artists, all dimensions {width="65%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Era averages by dimension {width="35%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Row {height="40%"} ### Strongest dimension by style {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Top 20 — composite score (High confidence) {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # 4 · Uncertainty in Practice ## Row {height="15%"} ::: {.callout-important title="Teaching Prompt — Communicating Uncertainty"} **Most dashboards show you the number. This one shows you how much to trust it.** The confidence flag is almost never present in executive reporting — but it should be. Where in your organization's reporting does uncertainty get hidden? What decisions are being made on L-confidence data presented as H-confidence? ::: ## Row {height="45%"} ### Composite distribution by confidence tier {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### Score range: What we don't know {width="50%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ## Row {height="40%"} ### Confidence notes — M and L entries {width="100%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # 5 · Visualization Design ## Row {height="15%"} ::: {.callout-tip title="Teaching Prompt — The Same Data, Different Stories"} **Every visualization choice is an argument.** The four charts below show the same underlying data — composite scores by era — rendered four different ways. Each emphasizes something different and hides something else. Which one is most honest? Which is most persuasive? Are those the same chart? ::: ## Row {height="85%"} ### A: Ranked bar {width="25%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### B: Time trend line {width="25%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### C: Distribution (violin) {width="25%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: ### D: Scatter with n shown {width="25%"} ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: ::: # 6 · Full Data Table ## Row ::: {.cell} ::: {.cell-output-display} ```{=html}
``` ::: :::
